petitioner_background: The petitioner is a singer from Armenia seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under EB-1A. Her career includes entering music school in 1987 (graduating 1994 in violin), singing in a junior ensemble from 1986, working as a singer in a band from 1995-1997, serving as a vocalist at a theater in Armenia from 1997-2003, and as a vocal teacher from 2001-2002. She earned a bachelor's degree in Vocal Art/Variety-Jazz Singing in 2009, a master's degree in 2010, and completed postgraduate studies with honors in 2012. Since 2014, she has worked as a Lecturer at the Jazz and Pop Department of a Vocal-Theory Faculty.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner offered about three dozen articles covering 2006-2018 reporting on her and her work as a singer.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined the petitioner met this criterion at the initial evidentiary stage. However, in the final merits determination, the AAO found that while the published material indicated some attention to her and her work, the petitioner did not establish that such reporting was consistent with a level of success consistent with being among the small percentage at the very top of the field. The AAO noted her limited media coverage averaged only 2-3 articles per year and she did not show how this compared to other singers among the very top of the field, nor did it reflect a 'career of acclaimed work in the field.'

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence showing the petitioner participated on the jury for a program on Armenia TV, and served as a judge for three student festivals/contests.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined the petitioner met this criterion at the initial evidentiary stage. However, in the final merits determination, the AAO found that her judging experience involved evaluating the work of amateurs and aspiring singers rather than nationally or internationally renowned artists. The petitioner did not show how her experience in evaluating aspiring singers and students compared to others at the very top of the field, nor did she demonstrate a consistent history of reviewing or judging recognized, acclaimed singers or experts in her field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Screenshots and evidence of the petitioner performing at various venues, including a venue in California, a Sports and Concerts Complex in Armenia, and a venue in France. Also included evidence that 1,233 of 1,381 seats were sold at her 2007 California concert and 2,041 seats were sold at her 2012 concert at the Sports and Concerts Complex in Armenia.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined the petitioner met this criterion (display) at the initial evidentiary stage. However, in the final merits determination, the AAO noted that simply displaying one's work does not automatically place one at the very top of the field. The petitioner did not demonstrate the significance of her performances, such as whether they brought wide praise from critics, drew notable crowds, raised attendance, or were responsible for the success or standings of the events. Regarding commercial success, she did not compare her box office receipts or ticket sales to others among the very top of the field, nor did she demonstrate that her work was regularly seen at highly reputable venues.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence reflecting receipt of about two dozen awards from 2003 to 2019, including 'Revelation of the Year' and 'Hit of the Year' at the First National Music Awards in Armenia, with accompanying articles reporting on the winners.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: While discussed in the final merits determination (not as a criterion the petitioner claimed to meet at the initial stage per the Director's findings), the AAO found that although the awards showed some recognition of her work, she did not demonstrate how they indicated she was among the small percentage at the very top of the field. The majority of the awards were recently established or created. Notably, an article from aravot.am stated 'almost all the participants were given something, probably not to celebrate the New Year offended,' undermining the prestige of the awards.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence reflecting that the petitioner earned approximately $4,200 for a two-hour concert.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not establish that she commanded earnings commensurate with sustained national or international acclaim. She did not demonstrate how her salary compared to others at the very top of her field, or that she received notoriety or attention based on her earnings separating herself from others in the field or placing her in the upper echelon.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal on de novo review. Although the petitioner met the initial evidentiary threshold of at least three criteria (published material, judging, and display), the final merits determination concluded she did not demonstrate sustained national or international acclaim or that she had risen to the small percentage at the very top of the field of endeavor. The AAO found her awards were largely from recently established competitions, her media coverage was limited (2-3 articles per year), her judging involved amateurs rather than acclaimed artists, her performances lacked demonstrated significance in terms of critical praise or attendance, recommendation letters were from a solicited few without establishing broader field recognition, and her earnings were not shown to be commensurate with top-of-field status. The record did not establish a 'career of acclaimed work in the field' as contemplated by Congress. The AAO declined to reach the issue of whether she intended to continue work in her area of extraordinary ability.
legal_citations: Matter of Christo's, Inc., 26 I&N Dec. 537, 537 n.2 (AAO 2015)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953, 954 (Assoc. Comm'r 1994)
Hamal v. Dep't of Homeland Sec. (Hamal II), No. 19-cv-2534, 2021 WL 2338316 (D.D.C. June 8, 2021)
Hamal v. Dep't of Homeland Sec. (Hamal I), No. 19-cv-2534, 2020 WL 2934954 (D.D.C. June 3, 2020)
Lee v. Ziglar, 237 F. Supp. 2d 914, 918 (N.D. Ill. 2002)
Matter of L-A-C-, 26 I&N Dec. 516, 526 n.7 (BIA 2015)
